Farn Polrrrcnl PRACTTcEs CouurssroN
428 J Street . Suite 620 r Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 o Fax (916) 322-0886
Iuly 26,2007
Mitchell Ing, City Council
City of Monterey Park
320 West Newmark Avenue
Monterey Park, Califomia 9 17 5 4-289 6
RE: Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our FiIe No.I-07-116
Dear Mr. Ing:
This letter is in response to your request for advice regarding your duties as a
Monterey Park City Councilmember and a member of the Monterey iark Redevelopment
Agency under the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Re-form Act (the ..Act,).r
Because your request is general in nature and does not involve a specific action pending
before your agencies on which you serve, we treat your request as one for informal
assistance.2 Also, please note ihat our advice is based sotely on the provisions of the
Act. We therefore offer no opinion on the application, if any, of other conflict-of-interest
laws such as cofilmon law conflict of interest or Government Code Section 1090.
QUESTTON
May you participate in the following decisions despite your community property
interest in income your spouse receives from Southem caiifomia Edison?
(1) Decisions on a proposed shopping center.
(2) Decisions on attomey bills for legal advice regarding the shopping center.
I Government Code sections 81000-91014. Commission regulations appeff attitle 2,sectiors
18109-18997, of the California Code of Regulations.
' Pl"u." note that informal assistarice does not provide the requestor with immunity provided by an
opinion or formal written advice. (Section 83114; Regulation lg32g@te),enclosed.)


File No. I-07-116
Page No.2
CONCLUSION
If Southem Califomia Edison is directly involved in any decision (as discussed
below), you will have aconflict of interest in the decision if there is any financial effect
on Southern California Edison. If Southern California Edison is indirectly involved, you
will only have a conflict of interest if the decision affects Southern California Edison as
follows:
"(A) The govemmental decision will result in an increase or
decrease to the business entity's gross revenues for a fiscal year in the
amount of $500,000 or more; or,
"(B) The governmental decision will result in the business entity
incurring or avoiding additional expenses or reducing or eliminating
existing expenses for a fiscal year in the amount of $200,000 or moie; or,
"(c) The governmental decision will result in an increase or
decrease in the value of assets or liabilities of $500,000 or more.,,
FACTS
You are a Monterey Park City Councilmember and a member of the Monterey
Park Redevelopment agency. Your spouse is a salaried employee of Southern California
Edison (SCE).
The city is considering a 500,000 square foot retail shopping center. SCE has
provided easements to grant public roadway access and parking-for the proposed
shopping center. The easements and payment to SCE wis decided by the city without
your involvement prior to you taking office
You now wish to know if you can make decisions regarding the shopping center
despite SCE's involvement. Specifically you reference tne foflowing decisi,ons:
o Decisions on the shopping center.
o Decisions on attorney bills for legal advice regarding the shopping center.
Your spouse is not representing SCE in connection with the easements or the
project.


File No. I-07-tt6
Page No. 3
ANALYSIS
The conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act require disqualification from any
decision in which you have a conflict of interest. This must be determined on a decision-
by-decision basis. In order to determine wfgthel trr" f-rritition in section szroo applies
to a given decision, Regulation 18700 provides the foilowing eight-st"f uruty.i..
Step One: Are you a o,public official?r,
As a member of the city council and redevelopment agency,you are a ..member,
officer, employee, or consultant of a state or local govemment agency,,and, therefo re, are
a "public official" subject to the conflict-of-interesiprovisions oittr" aJllsection
82048; Regulation I S70 1 (a).)
step Two: Are you making, participating in making, or influencing a
governmental decision?
A public offic11l"makes a govenrmental decision" when the official , acting
within the authority of his or her office orposition, votes on a matter, obligates or
commits his or her_lgency to any course olaction, o, "nt"r, into any conkactual
agreement on behalf of his or her agency. (Regulati on rg702.r.) Apublic officiar
"parlicipates in a governmental decision'*t"i', actin! *ittio tt" ""Ai;;;y "irri, orher
position and without significant substantive or interveiing review, the official negotiates,
advises or makes recommendations to the decisionmaker?egaraing the governmental
decision' (Regulation 1s702.2.) A public official ir utt"-ptirg to use his or her official
position to influence the decision if, for the purpose oiinfluencing the decision, the
official contacts, or appegs-before any T*iuo, officer, employee, or consultant of his or
her agency' Attempts to influence incrude, but are not timitea to, .,appearances or
contacts by the official on behalf of a business entity, client, or customer.,, (Regulation
18702'3(a)') You state in your letter that you will be making decisions related to the
shopping center and the payment of attomey's fees in connection wit legal advice on the
shopping center. Thus, you wourd be making *a purti"iputing in a decision.
Please note, if you have a conflict of interest, you are also disqualified from
influencing your agency on the decision. where u gorr"*ental decision will have
a reasonably foreseeable, material financial gffect on yo,i, ,"onomic interest, you may
not make contacts with, appear before, or otherwis " itt"iptto influenco, ffiy member,
9-fficer, employee, or consultant of your agency concerning the governmental decision.
(Regulation 18702.3(a).) This prohibition arso applies to iny agency appointed by or
subject to the budgetary control of the official,s il.*r. cegulation 1g702.3(a).)
If a public official's office is listed in Section 87200 (*g7200 filers,, include city
council members) and he or she has a conflict of interest in a decision noticei at apublic
meeting, then he or she must: (1) immediately prior to th" di."ussion of the item, verbally


File No. I-07-116
Page No. 4
identiff each tlpe of economic interest involved in the decision as well as details of the
economic interest, as discussed in Regulation 18702.5OX1)(B), on the record of the
meeting; (2) recuse himself or herself; and (3) leave the room for the duration of the
discussion andlor vote on the item. For closed sessions, consent calendars, absences and
speaking as a member of the public regarding personal interests, special rules found in
Regulation 78702.5, subdivisions (c) and (d) apply. (Section S7105.)
Step Three: Do you have economic interests?
A public official has a financial interest in a decision within the meaning of
Section 87103 if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have amateial
financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on the official, a
member of his or her immediate family, or on any one of five enumerated economic
interests. (Section 87103; Regulations 18703-18703.5.) Section 87103 sets forththe
following economic interests:
1. An interest in a business entity in which a public official has a direct or indirect
investrnent of $2,000 or more. (Section 37103(a); Regulation 18703.1(a).) An interest in
any business entity in which a public oflicial is a director, officer, partner, trustee, 
.
employee, or holds any position of management. (Section 87103(d); Regulation
18703.1(b).)
2. An interest in real property in which a public official has a direct or indirect
interest of $2,000 ormore. (Section 87103(b); Regulation 18703.2.)
3. Any source of income, including promised income, to the public official that
aggregates to $500 or more within 12 months prior to the decision. (Section 87103(c);
Regulation 18703.3.) "fncome" is defined to include any community property interest in
the income of a spouse.
4. Arry source of gifts to the public official if the gifts aggregate to $390 or more
within 12 months prior to the decision. (Section 87103(e); Regulation 19703.4.)
5. A public official also has an economic interest in his or her personal expenses,
income, assets, or liabilities, as well as those of his or her immediate family. This is also
known as the "personal financial effects" rule. (Section 87103; Regulation 18703.5.)
Your question solely concerns your community interest in the income of your
spouse from Southern California Edison. This letter does not consider any other
economic interests that might exist.


File No. I-07-116
Page No. 5
Step Four: Are your economic interests directly or indirectly involved in the
decision?
The next three steps, when considered together, identiftwhether a given decision
will have a reasonably foreseeable material finan-cial eifect on your economic interest.
This is the statutory test for disqualification. The first step is determining whether your
economic interest is directly or indirectly involved in a decision. This isin important
question because if your interest is directly involved, generally a strict rule for materiality
applies.
A person, including business entities, sources of income, and sources of gifts, is
directly involved in a decision before your agency when that person, either Jirectly or by
an agent:
'(1) Initiales the proceeding in which the decision will be made by
filing an application, claim, appeal, or similar request or;
',(2)Is alamed party in,_oris the subject of, theproceeding
concerning the decision before the official oith" officiai,s ug""tl e
person is the subjgct of a proceeding if a decision involves G i.rrr*.",
renewal, approval, denial, or revocation of any license, permit, or other
entitlement to, or contract with, the subject person.,, G"g,rlutio,
1870a.1@).)
Your facts suggest that Southern California Edison may be a named partyin some
of your agency's upcoming decisions. For example, SCE mayreceive entry fees for
temporary construction work on the easements, *t i"t require entry onto ScE property.
obviously, such decisions on the easements would directly invot# scE. 
- --
other decisions on the shopping center would not directly involve scE so long as
SCE is not an applicant, named party,or the subject of thedecision. This would also be
true with respect to attorney's fee decisions sincl the subject of those decisions would be
your attorney, not SCE. If a business ""1ity is not directly involved in a governmental
decision, it is deemed indirectly involved. lsee Regulation 1g704.1(b).)
Steps Five and Six: Will the financial effect of the decision on your economic
interest be material and reasonably foreseeable?
once a public official identifies his or her relevant economic interests, the official
must evaluate whether itjs_ reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material
financial effect on any of those economic interests. This determination takes two steps.
First, the official must find and apply the applicable materiality standard set forth in
commission regulations. (Regulation rg700(bx5), Regulation 1g705, .t ,"q.;-eto
finding the applicable materiality standard, the offiiial iaust then decide whether it is


File No. I-07-116
Page No. 6
reasonably foreseeable that the effect of the decision will equal or exceed thatmateiality
standard. (Regulation 1S700(bX6).)
Decision lireclll Affecting SCE: For economic interests in business entities
directly involved in a decision the materiality standard is given at Regulation lg705.l(b)
which states, ". . .the financial effects of a government decision on a Iusiness entity
which is directly involved in the govglnment decision is presumed to be material.,, Thus,
if SCE is directly involved in the decisions regarding the project, you may not participate
in the decisions, if they wil have any foreseeubt" fiiurrrial effect on scE.
Decision Indilctly Affecting S-CE: with respect to a business entity that is a
source of income and is indirectly involved in a decision, the mateiality.iria*a is given
in regulation 18705.1(c). The standards 
_fol ryteriality under this regulation vary with
the size of the business. scE is a New york stock Exchange company (NysE). For
companies listed on the NYSE, the financial effect of a given decision are considered
material if:
"(A) The Soveflrmental decision will result in an increase or
decrease a^l: bl:hess entity's gross revenues for a fiscal year in the
amount of $500,000 or more; or,
"(B) The governmental decision will result in the business entity
incurring or avoiding additional expenses or reducing or eliminating
existing expenses for a fiscal year in the amount of $-zoo,ooo o, -o7"; or,
"(C) The governmental decision will result in an increase or
decrease in the value of assets or liabilities of $500,000 or more.,,
once a public official has determined the materiality standard that applies to his
or her economic interest, the next step is determining whether it is ..reasod'r,
foreseeable" thatthe standard will bomet. (Regdattn 1g706.) An effect on economic
interests is considered "reasonably foreseeableiif *rere is a substantial likelihood that it
will occur. (Regulation_ 18706(a).) A financial effect need not be certain to be
considered reasonably foreseeable, but it must be more than a mere possibility . (In re
Thorner (1975) 1 FPPC ops. 198.) Generally, each governmental decision must be
analyzed independently to determine if there *itt U" a-reasonably foreseeable material
financial effect on an official's economic interests. (In re owen (1976)2 Fppc ops.
77.)
Steps Seven and Eight: Exceptions
We have not gone on to discuss the lattertwo steps. Step seven is an exception
that applies where the reasonably foreseeable and material financial effect on the
official's economic interest is not distinguishable from the effect on the p;ii;;"n erally,
and step eight is an exception that applies when the official is legally."q,rirrJti


File No. I-07-116
Page No. 7
partiapate in the decision. You have not provide d *y facts to suggest that these
exceptions may apply.
If you have other questions on this matter,please contact me at (916) 322_5660.
Sincerely,
Scott Hallabrin
Counsel
W. Wallace
General Counsel
Division
Jwwjgl
Enclosure

considered reasonably foreseeable, but it must be more than a mere possibility . (In re
Thorner (1975) 1 FPPC ops. 198.) Generally, each governmental decision must be
analyzed independently to determine if there *itt U" a-reasonably foreseeable material
financial effect on an official's economic interests. (In re owen (1976)2 Fppc ops.
77.)
Steps Seven and Eight: Exceptions
We have not gone on to discuss the lattertwo steps. Step seven is an exception
that applies where the reasonably foreseeable and material financial effect on the
official's economic interest is not distinguishable from the effect on the p;ii;;"n erally,
and step eight is an exception that applies when the official is legally."q,rirrJti
partiapate in the decision. You have not provide d *y facts to suggest that these
exceptions may apply.

CONCLUSION: If Southem Califomia Edison is directly involved in any decision (as discussed
below), you will have aconflict of interest in the decision if there is any financial effect
on Southern California Edison. If Southern California Edison is indirectly involved, you
will only have a conflict of interest if the decision affects Southern California Edison as
follows:
"(A) The govemmental decision will result in an increase or
decrease to the business entity's gross revenues for a fiscal year in the
amount of $500,000 or more; or,
"(B) The governmental decision will result in the business entity
incurring or avoiding additional expenses or reducing or eliminating
existing expenses for a fiscal year in the amount of $200,000 or moie; or,
"(c) The governmental decision will result in an increase or
decrease in the value of assets or liabilities of $500,000 or more.,,

Suite 620 r Sacramento, CA 95814-2329 (916) 322-5660 o Fax (916) 322-0886 Iuly 26,2007 Mitchell Ing, City Council City of Monterey Park 320 West Newmark Avenue Monterey Park, Califomia 9 17 5 4-289 6 RE: Your Request for Informal Assistance Our FiIe No.I-07-116 Dear Mr. Ing: This letter is in response to your request for advice regarding your duties as a Monterey Park City Councilmember and a member of the Monterey iark Redevelopment Agency under the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Re-form Act (the ..Act,).r Because your request is general in nature and does not involve a specific action pending before your agencies on which you serve, we treat your request as one for informal assistance.2 Also, please note ihat our advice is based sotely on the provisions of the Act. We therefore offer no opinion on the application, if any, of other conflict-of-interest laws such as cofilmon law conflict of interest or Government Code Section 1090. QUESTTON May you participate in the following decisions despite your community property interest in income your spouse receives from Southem caiifomia Edison? (1) Decisions on a proposed shopping center. (2) Decisions on attomey bills for legal advice regarding the shopping center. I Government Code sections 81000-91014. Commission regulations appeff attitle 2,sectiors 18109-18997, of the California Code of Regulations. ' Pl"u." note that informal assistarice does not provide the requestor with immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Section 83114; Regulation lg32g@te),enclosed.) File No. I-07-116 Page No.2 CONCLUSION If Southem Califomia Edison is directly involved in any decision (as discussed below), you will have aconflict of interest in the decision if there is any financial effect on Southern California Edison. If Southern California Edison is indirectly involved, you will only have a conflict of interest if the decision affects Southern California Edison as follows: "(A) The govemmental decision will result in an increase or decrease to the business entity's gross revenues for a fiscal year in the amount of $500,000 or more; or, "(B) The governmental decision will result in the business entity incurring or avoiding additional expenses or reducing or eliminating existing expenses for a fiscal year in the amount of $200,000 or moie; or, "(c) The governmental decision will result in an increase or decrease in the value of assets or liabilities of $500,000 or more.,, FACTS You are a Monterey Park City Councilmember and a member of the Monterey Park Redevelopment agency. Your spouse is a salaried employee of Southern California Edison (SCE). The city is considering a 500,000 square foot retail shopping center. SCE has provided easements to grant public roadway access and parking-for the proposed shopping center. The easements and payment to SCE wis decided by the city without your involvement prior to you taking office You now wish to know if you can make decisions regarding the shopping center despite SCE's involvement. Specifically you reference tne foflowing decisi,ons: o Decisions on the shopping center. o Decisions on attorney
